author: gurukamath
content:
Why can't we just charge the warm/cold gas for accessing the target upfront? Instead of splitting it into warm upfront + a cold surcharge later.
We don't need to access the state to determine if the target is cold

author: draganrakita
content:
> We don't need to access the state to determine if the target is cold 
This would be first time in evm this is done too, in revm is_warm is a flag inside accounts hashmap, it is more optimized if we can do it in same place (loading and warm check). We could move warm cost later when loading happens?

author: draganrakita
content: On one hand this is not a big deal, on other we are charging 100gas for tload/tstore that is basically a hashmap access. So i look at every hashmap access as 100gas.

author: gurukamath
content: Ah. Ok. The split access gas cost (warm upfront + cold surcharge later) makes sense in that case.

author: gurukamath
content: However, does this then mean, that all account accessing opcodes should follow this pattern? Also, would the delegation target access gas cost have to happen in two steps?

author: spencer_tb
content: FYI this change is in our `forks/amsterdam` branch, just kept it out of the `bal-devnet-7` spec. We are moving towards `glam-devnet-6` spec now so would be nice to finalize on all the BAL changes asap 😄

author: jsign
content:
Spent a bit of time catching up with #2473 (my original PR) and #2900 (the new proposed variant by <@429719902363058177> / <@792404665068158998>  ).

A bit of context first: the original reason why I did #2473 was to delay as much as possible the `get_code(...)` of the bytecode that was going to be executed by a CALL-like opcode, such that if this never happened (e.g. due to OOG or stack depth overflow), we didn’t do that `get_code(...)`. This `get_code(...)` isn’t much relevant for BALs since pre-state bytecode isn’t part of BALs, but it was relevant for me since I was focusing on the execution witness for zkVMs where *it does* get included (basically, execution witness is BALs plus bytecode and extra nits). So, I wanted to avoid “bytecode waste” in the execution witness in these OOG/stack-overflow situations.
(All this mosty to 7702-delegated ones, since non-delegated still do `get_code(...)` to know if they are delegated for propoer gas charging)

From what I see in #2900, the problem I wanted to solve is still solved, so I’m happy reg my original motivation. So, from that angle, I’m fine with both.

author: jsign
content:
Forgetting about zkVMs and execution witnesses and just thinking which change makes more sense “in general”: I see #2473 as a narrower change and #2900 as a more general and cover other nice savings by moving earlier things that can fail. e.g. if we’ll get a stack depth overflow, it doesnt’ make senes to add a lot of stuff that #2473 still was okay adding.

I’m not part of an EL client team so I’m just thinking theoretically, but feels like although #2900 seems a bit bigger change, feels more elegant in putting the order of things in check and being more optimal for BALs. I would guess if #2900 happens, might worth double checking some border-case tests to see these new “savings” properly materialize (or I should say do not materialize) into the BAL (but I guess that’s obvious post-spec change work).
I also don't know how much of an impact this implies to existing EL codebases and if this is a big deal or not either -- so I guess that also should be accounted for the final decision.

Sorry for being verbose -- I'm repeating things that I think most people already know here, just doing it to double-check if I might be misunderstanding something.
